key_points: Atoms consist of a positively charged nucleus and negatively charged electrons revolving around it
Nucleus contains protons and neutrons; electrons occupy shells around nucleus
Atomic number (Z) determines the number of protons and identity of element
Mass number (A) = protons + neutrons; number of neutrons = A – Z
Electrons are arranged in shells (K, L, M, N) with maximum 2, 8, 18, 32 electrons respectively
Isotopes: Atoms of the same element with same Z but different A
Isobars: Atoms with same mass number but different atomic numbers
Bohr model: electrons revolve in fixed energy levels; energy difference between levels explains spectral lines
examples: Carbon-12 and Carbon-14 are isotopes (Z=6, A=12 & 14)
formulas: Number of neutrons = Mass number – Atomic number
Mass number = Number of protons + Number of neutrons
Maximum electrons in a shell = 2n² (n = shell number)
exam_tips: Use atomic number and mass number to calculate subatomic particles
Differentiate isotopes and isobars with examples
Draw electron arrangement using Bohr’s model for simple atoms
Remember maximum electrons in each shell for electron configuration
Understand relationship between energy levels and spectral lines
